title: Toe Fractures
content: "Most toe fractures are minimally displaced and require only taping to an adjacent toe (buddy taping). Toe fractures may result from dropping a heavy object on the toes or stubbing them. Pain

title: Overview of Fractures
content: Fractures may be open, closed, pathologic, or stress related. Open fractures have disrupted skin with bone exposed to the environment. Closed fractures have intact skin. Pathologic fractures occur when weak bone breaks under minimal force due to conditions like osteoporosis, cancer, infection, or bone cysts. Stress fractures result from repetitive moderate force and accumulated microtrauma. Healing involves three stages: inflammatory (hematoma forms and minor resorption occurs), reparative (callus forms and new blood vessels develop; immobilization is required), and remodeling (callus ossifies and bone rebuilds, allowing gradual return to movement). Healing speed depends on age and circulation; children heal faster, while diabetes or poor blood flow slows healing. Acute complications include bleeding leading to shock, vascular injury causing distal ischemia, nerve injuries (neurapraxia, axonotmesis, neurotmesis), pulmonary embolism (especially in hip or pelvic fractures), fat embolism (causing respiratory distress and rash), compartment syndrome (causing necrosis or paralysis), and infection such as osteomyelitis. Chronic complications include instability, stiffness, arthritis, nonunion, delayed union, malunion, osteonecrosis, and limb length discrepancy. Evaluation includes history (mechanism, pain, sound at injury), physical exam (circulation, nerve function, wounds, deformity, swelling, tenderness, nearby joints), and imaging (radiographs with two views, CT or MRI for complex or hidden fractures). Open fractures are classified by the Gustilo-Anderson system (Grades I–IIIC). Arteriography may be used for arterial injury, and EMG for persistent nerve symptoms.

title: Sports-Related Concussions
content: Sports-related concussion is a mild traumatic brain injury caused by head impact, often seen in sports involving collision such as football, rugby, ice hockey, and lacrosse. Symptoms include loss of consciousness, confusion, memory problems, dizziness, headache, nausea, vomiting, tinnitus, and sensitivity to light or noise. Many patients do not lose consciousness but show signs like confusion, slow responses, or balance issues. Diagnosis is clinical, and neuroimaging is only done to rule out more serious injury because concussions do not show structural brain damage. Pathophysiology involves excitotoxicity from excessive glutamate release causing neuronal dysfunction. Incidence in the U.S. ranges from 200,000 to 3.8 million cases yearly, with underreporting common. Repeat concussions are frequent in athletes and increase the risk of chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE), a neurodegenerative disorder similar to Alzheimer disease, causing memory loss, impaired judgment, mood changes, and parkinsonism. Second-impact syndrome is a rare but often fatal condition where a second concussion occurs before full recovery, causing rapid brain swelling and increased intracranial pressure with a mortality rate near 50%. Diagnosis involves clinical evaluation by trained personnel, using tools like SAC or SCAT5 for sideline assessment. CT scans are recommended for loss of consciousness, Glasgow Coma Score under 15, focal neurologic deficits, or mental deterioration. Neurocognitive testing may be used for prolonged symptoms or to compare against baseline scores. Treatment includes immediate removal from play, rest, and symptom management with acetaminophen. Patients should avoid schoolwork, driving, alcohol, and screen use during recovery. Family members should monitor for worsening symptoms such as decreased consciousness, neurologic deficits, vomiting, confusion, or seizures. Return to play follows a gradual protocol: light aerobic exercise, sport-specific training, noncontact drills, full-contact practice, and competition only if symptom-free at each stage. Patients must be asymptomatic for at least one week before returning to full play; those with severe symptoms should wait one month. Multiple concussions in a season require careful evaluation of continued participation risks. Prognosis is generally full recovery, though postconcussive symptoms may persist for months. CTE can cause progressive brain degeneration leading to death within 10–15 years. Key points: concussion causes transient brain dysfunction with or without loss of consciousness; symptoms may persist for weeks; athletes should be removed from play and evaluated; imaging is for severe cases; and gradual, symptom-free return is essential to prevent repeat injury.

title: Infected Bite Wounds of the Hand
content: A small puncture wound, especially from a human or cat bite, can cause significant injury to tendons, joint capsules, or cartilage. Human bites commonly occur from clenched fist injuries where a tooth punctures the metacarpophalangeal joint during a punch. Human oral bacteria include Eikenella corrodens, staphylococci, streptococci, and anaerobes. These wounds are often treated late, increasing infection severity. Animal bites, particularly cat bites, frequently involve Pasteurella multocida, staphylococci, streptococci, and anaerobes. Serious complications can include infectious arthritis and osteomyelitis. Diagnosis is clinical and supported by radiographs to detect fractures, teeth fragments, or other foreign bodies that could sustain infection, along with wound cultures. Signs of infection include localized redness and pain; tenderness along a tendon suggests spread to the tendon sheath, and severe pain with movement indicates joint or tendon sheath infection. Treatment includes surgical debridement with the wound left open and antibiotic therapy. Outpatient empiric antibiotics may include amoxicillin/clavulanate 500 mg orally three times daily or penicillin 500 mg four times daily combined with either cephalexin 500 mg four times daily or dicloxacillin 500 mg four times daily. In regions with MRSA prevalence, trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, clindamycin, doxycycline, or linezolid are alternatives. For penicillin-allergic patients, clindamycin 300 mg every six hours can be used. The hand should be splinted in a functional position (20-degree wrist extension, 60-degree metacarpophalangeal flexion, slight interphalangeal flexion) and elevated, but prolonged immobilization should be avoided to prevent stiffness. Noninfected bite wounds may require debridement and antibiotic prophylaxis at half the treatment dose.

title: Knee Sprains and Meniscal Injuries
content: Knee sprains and meniscal injuries result from trauma to ligaments or menisci, causing pain, swelling, instability, and sometimes locking. The most affected structures are the medial collateral ligament and anterior cruciate ligament. Injury mechanisms include inward (valgus) forces damaging the medial structures, outward (varus) forces affecting lateral ones, anterior or posterior forces injuring cruciate ligaments, and twisting under weight causing meniscal tears. Symptoms include swelling, tenderness over injured ligaments or joint lines, limited motion, and a popping sound in ACL tears. Effusion may be detected with the patellar tap (ballottement) test. Diagnosis is mainly clinical, with X-rays to rule out fractures and MRI reserved for unresolved or severe cases. The Ottawa knee rules guide when to take X-rays: age over 55, isolated patellar tenderness, fibular head tenderness, inability to flex knee 90°, or inability to bear weight for 4 steps. Stress testing distinguishes partial from complete tears but is delayed if swelling and pain are severe; reexamination 2–3 days later is more sensitive than MRI for meniscal and ACL injuries. The Lachman test best detects ACL tears. If large hemarthrosis or gross instability is present, vascular injury (especially popliteal artery damage) should be ruled out using ankle-brachial index and CT angiography. Treatment depends on severity: mild sprains are managed with protection, rest, ice, compression, and elevation (PRICE), and early mobilization; severe sprains may require immobilization or surgery. Large effusions can be aspirated to reduce pain and spasm. Severe 2nd-degree or most 3rd-degree sprains need casting for at least 6 weeks, and some ACL or medial collateral ligament tears require arthroscopic repair. Meniscal tears may need surgery if large, complex, or persistently symptomatic. Physical therapy aids recovery, but early mobilization is not advised for unstable injuries. Immobilizers should be used temporarily until orthopedic evaluation. Key points: stress testing is vital to assess tear severity; MRI is for unresolved or severe cases; vascular injury should be checked if instability or hemarthrosis is present; and early conservative management with PRICE is the first-line approach.
